properties.name: Ash House (Fort de la Frèniere)
properties.description: This North West Company "canoe fort" was established on the north shore of the Souris River, which was originally called Rivière St. Pierre by La Vérendrye. Located near present-day Hartney, the fort was strategically surrounded on three sides by the river for natural defense. It was built to challenge the growing competition of the Hudson’s Bay Company in the Assiniboine region. Astronomer David Thompson recorded its precise latitude, noting it was roughly 13.5 miles from where Plum Creek entered the Souris.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Bacon Ridge
properties.description: Located south of Lake Ebb and Flow and its associated reserve, this settlement recorded a population of ninety Métis in 1959. It was categorized as a community where the Métis lived on the "fringe" of an Indian Reserve. The area is historically significant as a site for traditional maple sugaring.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Baie St. Paul (Saulteaux Village)
properties.description: A French-Métis parish on the Assiniboine River between St. François Xavier and Poplar Point. Father Belcourt founded the mission to transition the Métis and Saulteaux to a sedentary lifestyle by providing plows and oxen to plant potatoes, corn, and oats. The settlement included a school led by the Nolin sisters, who were fluent in French, English, Ojibwa, and Cree.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Balsam Bay
properties.description: A Métis community located south of Grand Beach with a recorded history dating to 1884. It was named by Matilda Aerson (née Thomas). The community was known for its local cemetery, which provided free burial grounds for several surrounding settlements including Beaconia, Stony Point, and Belair.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Barrows (Baden, Powell, National Mills and Red Deer Lake)
properties.description: This area was settled by the Red River Métis in the late 1700s. Barrows is the largest of a cluster of five associated Métis communities situated on the northern edge of the Porcupine Forest. It served as a rapidly constructed lumber town for the Red Deer Lumber Company. By the mid-1920s, the mill operation ceased, leading to a decline in the town's prominence. The area is historically linked to the Fort Red Deer River (or Fort Rivière la Biche), a North West Company post founded by Hugh McGillis in 1794. In 1959, the census recorded 130 Métis in Baden, 40 in Barrows, 20 in National Mills, and 59 in Red Deer Lake.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Bas de la Rivière (Fort Alexander)
properties.description: Originally established as a wintering post and depot for the North West Company in 1732, this location was vital because the soil was suitable for agriculture when other areas failed. In 1792, Simon Fraser and Lesieur Toussaint established a larger post here, also known as Sieurs Fort. It served as a strategic provisioning hub where bags of pemmican from the upper Assiniboine were stored for canoe brigades traveling to the far northwest. It was the unofficial capital of the NW Company's Lake Winnipeg district. By 1817, the Métis staff were farming wheat, barley, and potatoes, effectively supplying the Red River Settlement with seed grain during years of crop failure.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Bas de la Rivière (Pine Falls, Fort Alexander)
properties.description: This community spanned both shores of the Winnipeg River, from Pointe au Foutre (the portage at Pine Falls) down to Traverse Bay on Lake Winnipeg. It was ideally positioned for the October whitefish fishery, where fish spawn on the gravel bottoms of the bay. By the early 1800s, it featured extensive gardens that sold vegetables to incoming settlers. After the amalgamation of the fur companies in 1821, the site lost its status as a major transshipment hub but remained a thriving Métis agricultural and fishing community. In 1958, the road allowance community at Pine Falls alone recorded a population of 300 Métis.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Batoche
properties.description: A destination for French-speaking Métis who moved permanently to their traditional wintering quarters after the 1869-70 Resistance. It is recognized as a significant community in the Métis Homeland.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Battle River Valley
properties.description: Battle River (Notikiwin Seppe in Cree) originates south from Battle Lake in central Alberta and flows east into Saskatchewan, discharging into the North Saskatchewan River at Battleford. The Métis families of the Salois and Laboucanes settled in the valley in the mid-1800s, establishing trade routes using Red River carts and raising livestock. During the 1870s and 1880s, the Battle River valley became a corridor for Métis settlement at Laboucane (Duhamel), Saint-Paul-des-Métis, and the Battleford area.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Saskatchewan Métis Communities: Historical Overview. Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: Berens River
properties.description: Located on the east shore of Lake Winnipeg, this site was a stopping point for Métis boatmen since 1765. It was a thriving community until 1970, when a ban on fishing due to mercury contamination devastated the local economy. Most fishermen were forced to relocate, leaving the remainder dependent on government compensation.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Big Eddy (Settlement)
properties.description: A road allowance community located seven kilometers northwest of The Pas. When The Pas was incorporated in 1912, Big Eddy was intentionally excluded from the town boundaries. This exclusion allowed the municipality to deny Métis residents access to basic services like water and sewer.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Birds Hill
properties.description: Named after James Curtis Bird, an HBC Chief Factor who received a 3,000-acre land grant here in 1824. Known as "the Pines," its high ground served as a vital sanctuary for Red River settlers and their livestock during the great floods of 1826 and 1852.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Birsay Village (Orkney Town)
properties.description: Built west of Fort Douglas by Orkney-Cree Michif freemen seeking a sedentary life. Named for the home parish of leader Magnus Spence. The village was hit by outbreaks of whooping cough and measles, followed by a plague of grasshoppers that destroyed their crops.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Black Butte (Butte Noir)
properties.description: Black Butte was a camping and meeting place for the Dakota and Metis buffalo hunters. It is located NE of Velva, North Dakota on the big bend of the Souris River. The Buffalo Hunt of September 1860 is described in a report in the Nor'Wester, November 15, 1860, page 3. They note that the Metis camped at Butte Noir.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Black Butte (Butte Noir). Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: Blood River Post (Bloodvein River)
properties.description: An HBC post located at the mouth of the Bloodvein River on Lake Winnipeg. It was part of the extensive northern trade network utilized by Métis boatmen.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Blueberry Patch
properties.description: Traditional harvesting grounds within the Swan Pelican Provincial Forest used by the Métis and First Nations people of Pine Creek, Camperville, and Duck Bay. Located on unoccupied Crown lands, these grounds were essential for food and medicinal gathering.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Boggy Creek and San Clara
properties.description: Settled by fur trade families in the 1880s, these hubs near Roblin were thriving communities for trapping and trade. In the late 1930s, many founding families (Jeromes, Langans) were forcibly displaced under the Prairie Farm Rehabilitation Act to create community pastures.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Brandon House and Fort Assiniboine
properties.description: Brandon House was founded by the HBC on the northeast bank of the Assiniboine River. It was a central trade hub but underwent several moves due to competition. In 1816, it was famously captured and plundered by Cuthbert Grant and a party of Métis as a prelude to the Battle of Seven Oaks.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Buffalo Lake
properties.description: Buffalo Lake and the Battle River Valley in present day Alberta came to prominence as Métis gathering places after the great small-pox epidemic (la picotte) of 1870. The Métis fled from St. Albert, Lac St. Anne and Edmonton to escape the disease. The Buffalo Lake site is located between Lynn and Buffalo Lakes southeast of Edmonton, part of a network of four Métis wintering sites including Salois' Crossing near Duhamel, Tail Creek near Boss Hill, Todd's Crossing near Ponoka, and Dried Meat Hill.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Buffalo Lake and the Battle River Valley. Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: Butte St. Paul ("Ot Accowabiwinins")
properties.description: Butte St. Paul ('Ot Accowabiwinins') is located northwest of Dunseith, North Dakota, on the Turtle Mountain escarpment. It was named by missionary George Anthony Belcourt, who survived a blizzard there in January 1850 and celebrated Mass on the feast of the Conversion of St. Paul (Jan 25). Belcourt blessed a large wooden cross on the summit, and a commemorative cairn was dedicated in 1933.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Butte St. Paul. Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: Calf Mountain (Tete de Boeuf), Little Dance Hill
properties.description: Located south of Darlingford with an elevation of 1,550 feet, this was a sacred ceremonial site for Plains Indians who called it Mepawaquomoshin (Little Dance Hill). It was a strategic landmark for Red River buffalo hunters. In the mid-1850s, a legendary battle occurred here where 1,500 buffalo hunters defeated a Sioux war party; roughly 597 warriors were reportedly buried on the western slope. In 1909, an anthropologist from the University of Toronto dug into the mound, removing many artifacts to the dismay of local Aboriginal people.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Camperville
properties.description: History is rooted in the 1860s salt trade where Métis prepared salt for the HBC. Renamed for Father Camper, who encouraged Métis from St. Laurent to settle there. A residential school was established in 1894 to educate Indian and Métis children.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Carlton Trail (Métis Stopping Points)
properties.description: A network of stopping houses established as Métis freighted goods west to Edmonton. These houses, such as High Bluff and Tanner’s Crossing, evolved into small Métis settlements.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Carrot River Valley (Pasquia Settlement)
properties.description: A strategic region south of The Pas used for farming and gathering. It includes settlements like Young's Point and was historically a rendezvous for hunters awaiting spring fish runs.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Catfish Creek
properties.description: Flows into the Assiniboine River near present-day Polo Park. Historically significant as the location where Cuthbert Grant and his men left the river to move north to Frog Plain in 1816.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Cayer
properties.description: A Métis community located northeast of McCreary and east of Dauphin. Named for Narcisse K. Cayer, it had a population of 150 Métis residents in 1959.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Cedar Lake (Chemanwawin) Métis Settlement
properties.description: A self-sufficient community on the west shore of Cedar Lake, its name derived from the Cree word for "a place for fishing with nets". In the early 1960s, the entire settlement—composed of 43 First Nations and 12 Métis families—was flooded out by the Grand Rapids Hydro Project. They were relocated to Easterville on a townsite that was deemed unsuitable for their traditional subsistence lifestyle. The community received only $20,000 in compensation and vague, unfulfilled promises of economic aid from the provincial government.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Charleswood (St. Charles Parish)
properties.description: The location of "The Passage," a natural ford for Red River carts. Among the earliest settlers on the river lot farms were the Beauchemin, Hogue, and Morrissette families.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Choteau
properties.description:
Métis families who arrived from Canada after the failed Riel rebellion of 1885 were among the earliest settlers along northern Montana's Front Range around Choteau. Métis families hid in the foothills and canyons due to fears of deportation.
Source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Front Range Métis. Louis Riel Institute.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Front Range Métis (Choteau). Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: Cold Lake
properties.description: A road allowance community south of Sherridon known as "Moccasin Flats". Residents worked in mining and guiding but were denied land titles. Senator Ed Head recalled rebuilding log homes further down the road whenever they were forced to move.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Cold Lake (Kississing)
properties.description: A road allowance community south of Sherridon known as "Moccasin Flats". Residents worked in mining and guiding but were denied land titles. Senator Ed Head recalled rebuilding log homes further down the road whenever they were forced to move.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Cooks Creek
properties.description: Site of St. Peter’s Anglican Mission established on land donated by Chief Peguis. By 1850, it was a productive community with 500 residents living on narrow river lots practicing agriculture.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Cormorant
properties.description: Located north of The Pas on Cormorant Lake, this is a historic Indigenous campsite and fishing area. In 1959, it had 169 Métis residents and was key to managing the Summerberry Marsh trapping.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Cranberry Portage
properties.description: A 2,000-year-old portage between the Grassy River and Lake Athapapuskow. Métis trapper David Collins is credited with showing prospectors the claim that became the Flin Flon mines.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Crane River (O-Chi-Chak-Ko-Sipi)
properties.description: Located on the northwest shore of Lake Manitoba. Historically, Métis and First Nations gathered here to collect salt. It borders the Crane River First Nation.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Crescentwood
properties.description: Originally composed of long Métis river lots south of the Assiniboine River in Winnipeg. Wealthy development starting in 1902 displaced original residents, pushing them toward Rooster Town.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Cumberland House
properties.description: A major fur trade juncture established in 1774. It was the main route into the North in the late 1800s.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Curling River Post
properties.description: Established by Peter Fidler's son Charles to compete with NWC posts near Minnedosa. It was located on the Curling (now Rolling) River.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Cypress Hills
properties.description: A vital seasonal hunting ground and settlement area for the plains Métis. Hivernant families and brigades utilized the area extensively.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.
properties.review_note: No supporting source found in local archive — Virtual Museum only

properties.name: Deer Rapids
properties.description: The location of early Indian and Métis settlements on the Red River, later known as St. Andrews.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Dog Head Settlement
properties.description: Located on the east shore of Lake Winnipeg; home to itinerant Métis such as prospector Louis Simard Sr.. Simard held claims in several mining shafts like the San Antonio.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Dog Patch
properties.description: A road allowance community in Winnipeg near the CPR Weston shops. Created as Métis were displaced by industrial expansion and urban development.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Duck Bay (Baie de Canard)
properties.description: A centuries-old gathering place on Lake Winnipegosis known for salt springs and fisheries. Many members took scrip in 1885 but unsuccessfully petitioned to return to Treaty status in 1893.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Duck Mountain
properties.description: San Clara and Boggy Creek are located north of Roblin, Manitoba and west of the Duck Mountain Provincial Forest. Prior to the 1870's, the Cree, Ojibway and Métis First Nations inhabited the Roblin area. Early settlement patterns were intimately linked to the fur trade and related transportation networks. The Pelly Trail and the Shell River facilitated the marketing of furs hunted in the Duck Mountains. In the early 1880's, the Métis concentrated their land claims around San Clara and Boggy Creek.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba and Geographical Place Names. Louis Riel Institute, 2018.

properties.name: Easterville
properties.description: The relocation site for families from the flooded Cedar Lake community. The townsite was deemed unsuitable for their traditional way of life, leading to government dependency.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Eddystone
properties.description: Originally called Lonely Lake; in 1938, residents collaborated to build the St. Thomas Aquinas Catholic Church. Land was donated by Jim Spence.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Edmonton House (Fort Augustus)
properties.description: Fort Edmonton (also called Fort-des-Prairies by the Métis and Amiskwaskahegan — 'Beaver Hills House' — by the Cree) was the end point of the Carlton Trail, the main overland route for Métis freighters between Red River Settlement and the west. Built by the HBC in 1795 near the NWC's Fort Augustus, it had 60–80 Métis employees living nearby with their families. The fort was moved upstream to present-day downtown Edmonton in 1801 due to lack of firewood, and later to the site of the Alberta Legislature after floods in the late 1820s.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Edmonton House. Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: Fairford Settlement (formerly Pinaymootang)
properties.description: A mission and trading post where trade relations were notoriously tense in the late 1700s. Duncan Cameron wintered there in 1795-96.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Fish Creek
properties.description: Flows into the Assiniboine River near present-day Polo Park. Historically significant as the location where Cuthbert Grant and his men left the river to move north to Frog Plain in 1816.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Flee Island Entrenchments
properties.description: Fortified camps built by Dakota refugees in 1864. They featured circular trenches and pits where women and children took refuge during attacks by bounty hunters.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Fort Alexander (Sagkeeng)
properties.description: Originally an HBC post built to counter the NWC's Bas-de-la-Rivière. It was a key provisioning post where pemmican from the upper Assiniboine was stored for northwestern brigades.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Fort Dauphin
properties.description: Established in present-day Winnipegosis by Chevalier de la Vérendrye. Explorer Peter Fidler died here in 1822. Later known as "Dogtown".
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Fort de la Rivière Tremblante (Grant’s House, Aspin House)
properties.description: Built by Robert Grant of the North West Company in 1791, this post was situated on the Upper Assiniboine River near the mouth of the Rivière Tremblante. It became the favorite residence of Cuthbert Grant Sr. when he took charge of the district in 1793. The fort functioned as both a source of high-quality furs and a provisioning depot for brigades heading to Athabasca country. Grant abandoned the post in 1798 due to extreme competition from 14 other rival posts in the immediate region.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Fort Desjarlais
properties.description: Built by Joseph Desjarlais and protected by an oak palisade. It was a major supplier of pemmican and whiskey.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Fort Ellice
properties.description: Established on the Qu’Appelle River; it served as the headquarters for the Swan River District. Managed by famous Métis traders like the McKays.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Fort Gibralter
properties.description: Built at the Forks by the NWC; seized by Louis Riel in 1869. Site for meetings of the Legislative Assembly of Assiniboia.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Fort la Reine (Portage la Prairie)
properties.description: Established near a buffalo crossing and sturgeon fishery. Home of Chief Peichito Tanner, who operated 250 Red River carts.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Fort Langley
properties.description:
Fort Langley was established on the Fraser River by Chief Trader James McMillan in 1827, assisted by his clerks including François Annance (Métis), Donald Manson, and George Barnston. The post had an excellent salmon fishery and began an export lumber trade. It was rebuilt 4 km upstream in 1839. During the 1858 Gold Rush, Fort Langley was the departure point for prospectors and the site of the Crown Colony proclamation.
Source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Fort Langley. Louis Riel Institute.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Fort Langley. Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: Fort Montagne à la Bosse
properties.description: Built on the Upper Assiniboine near buffalo wintering grounds. Buffalo could be seen grazing from the fort gate at almost any season.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Fort Mr. Grant
properties.description: Built by Cuthbert Grant after the HBC closed Brandon House. Used to trade with Métis and Indians from Turtle Mountain to Qu’Appelle.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Fort Pelly
properties.description: Headquarters for the Swan River District before being replaced by Fort Ellice.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Fort Pinancewaywining
properties.description: Located south-west of Morden; first post supplied by Red River Carts from Pembina. Built by Alexander Henry the Younger.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Fort Rouge
properties.description: Located at the south point of the Forks; originally river lots owned by Métis families. Residents were displaced by suburban expansion.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Fort Souris
properties.description: Built on the Souris River for Mandan trade. Abandoned when HBC built Brandon House but later re-established.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Fort St. Charles
properties.description: Built by La Vérendrye in the early 1730s; ruins discovered in 1902. Contained remains of La Vérendrye's son.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Fouillard Town (The Corner / Li Kwayn)
properties.description: A road allowance community near Binscarth established by refugees from Ste. Madeleine. After their homes in Ste. Madeleine were burned in 1939 to make way for a community pasture, residents resettled on a former gravel pit that had once been part of the Gambler Reserve. The community was sarcastically named Selby Town after a municipal official responsible for their displacement. In 1946, the Fouillard Special School was built to replace the mission school they lost.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Four Persons
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture (Gabriel Dumont Institute)
properties.review_note: No supporting source found in local archive — Virtual Museum only

properties.name: Frog Plain (La Grenouillère)
properties.description: Traditional meeting place north of Seven Oaks. Site of annual rendezvous where partners met brigades bringing pemmican.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Gabriel's Crossing
properties.description: A historic ferry crossing on the South Saskatchewan River operated by Gabriel Dumont.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.
properties.review_note: No supporting source found in local archive — Virtual Museum only

properties.name: Grand Marais
properties.description: Community on the east shore of Lake Winnipeg that maintained exclusive Métis identity until the mid-1950s. Overwhelmed by the tourist industry.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Grand Rapids (Red River and Saskatchewan River)
properties.description: The Red River community was formed after 1823 north of Winnipeg on high ground. In 1840, it was 39% Métis fathers and 97% Aboriginal mothers. The Saskatchewan River site was a fur trade gateway at the river's mouth at Lake Winnipeg. It was a base for "free traders" and a gathering place for seasonal sturgeon fishing. In the 1960s, the provincial government bulldozed Métis homes to clear the area for a hydro project, forcing residents to accept minimal compensation.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Grande Pointe
properties.description: Métis settlement dating back to the 1860s along the Seine River. Residents found employment building the Pembina Branch Railway in the 1870s.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Grassy Narrows House
properties.description: HBC outpost on Lake Winnipeg staffed by Métis families like the Whiteways and Spences. Located on the west bank of the Hecla channel.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Green Lake (Lac Vert)
properties.description:
Green Lake (Lac Vert), Saskatchewan, was founded by the Métis in 1782. The site hosted Northwest Company and Hudson's Bay Company posts. Located 55 km northeast of Meadow Lake, it served as a key entrepôt between Fort Carlton and the Methye Portage. In 1940 the province established the Central Farm agricultural project to assist Métis residents.
Source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Green Lake, Saskatchewan. Louis Riel Institute.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Green Lake, Saskatchewan. Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: Hair Hills or Pembina Mountains
properties.description: Named for buffalo wool rubbed off on trees. Strategic wintering spot for hunters and site of early trading outposts.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Havre
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture (Gabriel Dumont Institute)
properties.review_note: No supporting source found in local archive — Virtual Museum only

properties.name: Îlets de Bois (now Carman)
properties.description:
Primary stopping point for buffalo hunters to repair carts. Famous for spring "sugar lots" where families tapped maple trees.

---

Winding river site famous for cart builders and "sugar lots". Now called Boyne River.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Island Cache
properties.description: Island Cache was located just before the forks of the Fraser and Nechako Rivers. It was a short-lived Métis road allowance-type community at the juncture of the Nechako and Fraser Rivers, separated from Prince George by a flood channel. The references tell the story of how after a severe flood in 1972, the community was bulldozed and the Métis were pushed out by the city of Prince George and the area was turned into an industrial zone.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Island Cache, British Columbia. Louis Riel Institute. References: Evans, Krebs et al. A Brief History of the Short Life of the Island Cache (2004); Evans and Foster. 'A Case of Genocide.' Shima, Vol. 4, No. 2, 2010.

properties.name: Jack River Post
properties.description: HBC post south of Norway House; relocation site for Selkirk Settlers in 1815. Replaced by modern Norway House site in 1827.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Kelly Lake
properties.description: Kelly Lake is British Columbia's only distinct Métis community. It was established around 1910 by Métis migrating west from Flying Shot, Alberta, marking the end of a century-long migration north and west from the Red River. Kelly Lake was chosen because it offered good water, wild hay, logs for cabins and fuel, and an abundance of moose and smaller game. Located 1.6 kilometres inside the BC border, 56 kilometres south of Dawson Creek, the community is predominantly Métis with families of Cree-Métis ancestry. The isolation of Kelly Lake has allowed its residents to retain their unique cultural identity, and the community has been pursuing land claims through the British Columbia Treaty Commission process.
properties.source: Dorion-Paquin, Paquin, and Young. The Kelly Lake, British Columbia Métis. Louis Riel Institute / Gabriel Dumont Institute.

properties.name: Kettle Stones
properties.description: Sandstone concretions in the Swan-Pelican forest. Traditional harvesting area for families of Pine Creek and Duck Bay.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Kinosota
properties.description: Established at the narrows of Lake Manitoba. Relocated in 1828 to oppose independent hunters. Treaty Two signed here in 1871.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: La Chevulure Montagne Post (Hair Hills Post)
properties.description: Outpost established by Alexander Henry the Younger in the early 1800s to serve hunters of the Pembina hills.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Laboucane (Duhamel)
properties.description: The Laboucane Settlement (later Duhamel Settlement) was founded in the early 1870s by François Gabriel Dumont, who moved from Lac St. Anne to the Battle River with his brother-in-law Abraham Salois and the Salois sons. The community was located at the Saddle Lake–Battleford crossing of the Battle River, between modern Wetaskiwin and Camrose. Six Laboucane brothers settled north and south of the river, and the Métis operated as buffalo hunters and later as farmers. In 1881 a mission was established and named Duhamel after Archbishop Thomas Duhamel of Ottawa.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Laboucane Settlement. Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: Lac des Chênes (Flat Creek)
properties.description: A traditional Métis wintering site that became a permanent settlement by the late 1860s, also known as Oak Lake.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Lac Ste. Anne
properties.description: Gabriel Dumont (1795–1880) and his extended kin group of bison hunters established Lac St. Anne west of Edmonton. Known as Manitou Sakahigan to the Métis, it became the site of an annual Métis religious pilgrimage. In 1844 a Catholic mission was set up — the first permanent Catholic mission west of Winnipeg. By 1887 the buffalo had disappeared and the lake lost importance as a gathering place, but Father Lestanc built a shrine and the first pilgrimage was held in 1889. The feast day of St. Anne is July 26.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Lac St. Anne, Alberta. Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: Lake Metigoshe Métis Settlement
properties.description: Ancient camping place on Turtle Mountain that became a wintering site for hunters from Grantown. First settlers included Louis Lee Racine.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Lane’s Post (St. François Xavier)
properties.description: Established in the 1850s at Pigeon Lake on the Assiniboine River, this post first appears in HBC records in 1856. It was managed by William Lane and was primarily used for its farming operations. The post was located about five miles past the St. François Xavier church. In 1864, it was the site where a plot was hatched to capture Dakota refugee leaders Little Six and Medicine Bottle. They were reportedly lured into a trap and drugged with wine before being bound and transported to Fort Pembina.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.
properties.review_note: No supporting source found in local archive — Virtual Museum only

properties.name: Last Mountain Lake
properties.description: Last Mountain Lake (also called Long Lake) was a well-known wintering site for Métis bison hunters. The Hudson's Bay Company built Last Mountain House at the southern end of the lake in 1869. After the post was destroyed by fire, a pair of hunting camps developed on the lake's south shore — one Métis and one First Nations. The area later became Canada's first federal bird sanctuary in 1887.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Last Mountain Lake. Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: Lido Plage
properties.description: Recreation area on the Assiniboine that hosted the annual "Old Timers & Pioneers Picnic". Featured jigging and fiddling contests.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Little Britain
properties.description: Early name for Lockport; settled by retiring Orkneymen and families. Known for its landmark Presbyterian church built by master stonemasons.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Little Chicago (Chicago Line)
properties.description: Chicago Line (Little Chicago) was a Métis road allowance community located 5–7 miles outside of Lestock, Saskatchewan, home to 15–20 families on marginal land of brush, sloughs, and swamp. Adults worked seasonally — brush cutting, stone picking, hay cutting, and picking Seneca root. Some children attended the Residential Mission Day School at Lestock. In 1949, the government relocated many families to the Métis Colony at Green Lake; a couple of years later, the government burnt down the road allowance houses at Chicago Line.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Métis Road Allowance Communities: Little Chicago or Chicago Line, Saskatchewan. Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: Little Grand Rapids House
properties.description: HBC post at Family Lake named for rapids on Berens River. Supplies were delivered by York boat until 1871.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Little Saskatchewan River
properties.description: Originates in Riding Mountain; called "Rivière Rapide" by David Thompson. Its valley was a key wintering area for bison.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Longbody Creek (Kenepikiniwewe)
properties.description: Established in 1988 for Métis displaced from Bloodvein Reserve. The community dispersed within five years as many returned to the reserve.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Loon Straits
properties.description: Fishing and trapping community on eastern shore of Lake Winnipeg. Residents renowned for building seaworthy oak and cedar boats.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Lorette (Petit Point des Chênes)
properties.description: Settled in 1850s by families from central parishes. A community of freighters and buffalo hunters whose cattle grazed along the Seine.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: MacGregor
properties.description: Road allowance community west of Portage la Prairie. Residents displaced by the rail line and later lobbied for housing land in 1961.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Manigotagan (Bad Throat River)
properties.description: Named for the sound of a waterfall like a bad sound in the throat. Surveyed into 18 river lots in 1904 for Métis laborers.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Manitoba House (Kinosota)
properties.description: See entry for Kinosota.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Mapleton
properties.description: Also called "Old England," home to fur traders and Chief Peguis’ people. Along the Red River north of Lower Fort Garry.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Matheson Island (formerly Snake Island)
properties.description: Stopping place for York boats renamed in 1903 after Daniel Matheson. Residents descend from Norwegian, English, and Icelandic settlers.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Meadow Portage
properties.description: Located on a narrow strip between Lake Manitoba and Lake Winnipegosis. Fur trade hub and later ranching community.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Metikewap (Wooden/Bark Tent)
properties.description: Road allowance settlement east of The Pas named for bark-covered teepees. Disappeared due to flooding from Grand Rapids dam.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Métis Island
properties.description: Site of a settlement north of Ross Island at Cross Lake before relocation to Nelson River.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Moose Lake (Settlement)
properties.description: Community southeast of The Pas whose traditional livelihood was devastated by dam flooding. Recorded 282 Métis in 1958.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Netley Creek (Rivière aux Morts)
properties.description: Site of native horticulture in 1805 where corn and potatoes were grown. HBC established a post here in 1813 with sixty horses.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Norway House and Rossville
properties.description: Transshipment hub where Chief Factors met in council. World-renowned center for building York boats.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Oak Lake (Lac des Chênes / Métis Wintering Sites)
properties.description: Watering stop west of Brandon; became a permanent settlement by late 1860s. Staffed by 50-100 Métis men.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Oak Point House and Oak Point
properties.description: HBC trading post and wintering place for cattle where Métis took land grants. Associated with the "Oak Point Nation".
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Opaskweyaw (The Pas)
properties.description: Rendezvous point for Cree and Métis hunters. Site of Devon Mission, established by Rev. Henry Budd in 1840.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Oxford House
properties.description: Established on the Hayes River route Midway between York Factory and Norway House. William Sinclair raised 12 children there.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Park Valley
properties.description: Park Valley (originally Nukeewin — 'The Stopping Place') was a Métis road allowance community in Saskatchewan where Maria Campbell grew up. In 1915, the creation of Prince Albert National Park displaced the Non-Status Indian and Métis families who lived there. The name Nukeewin was changed to Park Valley in 1925.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Park Valley, Saskatchewan. Louis Riel Institute. Citing Campbell, Maria. 'Kookoom Mariah and the Mennonite Mrs.' Journal of Mennonite Studies, Vol. 19, 2001.

properties.name: Pembina
properties.description: Named for buffalo wool rubbed off on trees. Strategic wintering spot for hunters and site of early trading outposts.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Petit Point de Roches
properties.description: Red River community north of Ste. Agathe inhabited by buffalo hunters and freighters.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Pikwitonei
properties.description: Railway division point where Métis engaged in track maintenance and sturgeon fishery. Division point until 1972.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Pine Bluff
properties.description: Self-sufficient settlement southeast of The Pas established as early as 1874. Entirely flooded by the Grand Rapids Dam.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Pine Dock (Bullhead)
properties.description: Community on Lake Winnipeg historically known as Bullhead.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Fort des Épinettes (Pine Fort)
properties.description: Lowest post on Assiniboine; chief trade hub for Mandan Indians. Abandoned in 1794.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Pointe à Grouette (Petite Pointe à Saline, St. Agathe)
properties.description: Settlement of hunters and salt makers where most were disenfranchised after 1870. Known as Petite Pointe à Saline.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Pointe Coupée (St. Adolphe)
properties.description: Red River parish founded by Métis in early 1800s. Site of 1869 Resistance action against surveyors.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Pointe des Chênes (Grande Pointe des Chênes, Ste. Anne de Chênes)
properties.description: Woodcutting center where residents were primary laborers for Dawson Road. Enraged by John Snow ignoring boundaries.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Pointe du Chien-Maigre
properties.description: As the Métis freighted west to Edmonton along the Carlton Trail a number of stopping houses were established and became small Métis settlements. These were: High Bluff, Totogan, White Mud now Westbourne, the Third Crossing, later called Palestine then Gladstone, and Tanner's Crossing.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba and Geographical Place Names. Louis Riel Institute, 2018.

properties.name: Poitras House
properties.description: HBC post near Brandon named for the Métis trader in charge.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Poplar Point
properties.description: Settlement featuring a log church with a bell from York Factory. Church completed in 1864.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Portage La Loche
properties.description: The Portage La Loche (Methye Portage) Brigade was organized in 1826, with men recruited from the Red River Settlement by the Hudson's Bay Company. The brigade traveled 4,000 miles annually by York boat, from Red River to Portage La Loche — the Height of Land separating waters flowing into the Arctic Sea from those draining into Hudson's Bay. Many of the boatmen were Métis, and many took part in the 1869 Red River Resistance. The brigade was broken into shorter sections after the Resistance.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. The Portage La Loche Brigade. Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: Prairie à Fournier
properties.description: Mission site east of St. Eustache on land gifted by Governor Simpson. Nolin sisters opened a school here in 1834.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Rabbit Point (Princess Harbour)
properties.description: Small peninsula on Lake Winnipeg near Bloodvein Bay. Positioned 304 air km north of Winnipeg.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Rall’s (Rahls) Island (Settlement)
properties.description: Road allowance community downstream from The Pas. Founded by Charles Rall and Elizabeth Knight.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Rat Portage
properties.description: Rat Portage (renamed Kenora in 1905) was the main trading post on the north side of Lake of the Woods in the Rainy Lake district. The name is a shortened corruption of Wauzhushk Onigum, the 'rat carrying place' — the major portage linking Lake of the Woods to the Winnipeg River. Métis managers of the post included James Isbister, Francois Mainville, Charles Goulait, and George McPherson. The post moved to the mainland in 1861. Rat Portage became the centre of the Ontario-Manitoba boundary dispute (1870–1884), with both provinces claiming it, each operating jails and issuing competing mining titles until the Privy Council decided for Ontario in 1884.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Rat Portage – Name changed to Kenora in 1905. Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: Rat River (Rivière aux Rats)
properties.description: Rat River (Rivière aux Rats, Wasushk Watapa) was a Métis wintering site located where the Rat River flows into the Red River. After the boundary line left Pembina in the United States, many Métis relocated to Canada. When they could not obtain a U.S. missionary, on March 2, 1825 the Bishop of Juliopolis wrote to Robert Pelly, Governor of Assiniboia, requesting land at Rat River near la Saline. The Rat River Métis Settlement surrounded the present community of St. Pierre Jolys. The area had plentiful hay lands and wood for making Red River carts.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Rivière aux Rats, St. Pierre Jolys, Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: Red Deer Lake
properties.description: This cluster of five Métis communities is located on the edge of the Porcupine Forest near the former Fort Red Deer River trading post. Barrows served as a major lumber town for the Red Deer Lumber Company until the sawmill closed around 1927. Baden was the largest of these communities in 1959 with 130 residents.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Red River Settlements (North of Upper Fort Garry)
properties.description: Network of narrow river lots extending north, home to hunters and farmers. Lots extended two miles east and west of river.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Riding Mountain
properties.description: Known as Manitouapau Hills or "Hill of the Buffalo Hunt". Sacred grounds for elk and a strategic migration route.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Rivière Esturgeon (St. Charles)
properties.description:
Métis community west of Sturgeon Creek with a buffalo crossing and ferry. Formally renamed St. Charles in 1854.

---

The location of "The Passage," a natural ford for Red River carts. Among the earliest settlers on the river lot farms were the Beauchemin, Hogue, and Morrissette families.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Rivière Gratchias (Morris)
properties.description: Rest stop for Red River carts making their way south to St. Paul. Named for the abundant burdock (gratchias).
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Rivière Sale (St. Norbert)
properties.description: Settlement dating back to 1822; cradle of the 1869-70 Resistance. Site of the famous wooden blockade turned back McDougall.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Rock Lake (Lac des Roches)
properties.description: Source of Pembina River; inhabited by descendants of Henry’s Métis employees. People intermarried with Nakoda and Chippewa Cree.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Rooster Town (Pakan Town)
properties.description: Last known road allowance community in Winnipeg, located between railway tracks. Named Pakan Town for hazelnuts. Evicted in 1959 to make way for a shopping mall and school.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Salt Point Métis Settlement
properties.description: A community stretching along a five-kilometre section of the east shore of Lake Winnipegosis, accessed via PR #276. In the 1800s, the Monkman family operated a large-scale salt-making enterprise here, preparing salt by evaporation for the Hudson's Bay Company.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Sandy Bay
properties.description: Originally the White Mud Band, these Ojibway-French mixed-bloods were relocated multiple times—first 20 miles north of Westbourne, then straight north in 1873, and finally to the current site in 1877 due to a surveyor’s error. In 1885, many members were pressured into withdrawing from Treaty to take scrip.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Sault à la Biche (Deer Rapids, St. Andrews)
properties.description: Located at the limestone rapids of the Red River south of Selkirk. It was chosen for early Indian and Métis settlements because the high ground offered protection from the massive floods that frequently devastated the Forks. The Selkirk Settlers later renamed the area St. Andrews.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Seven Oaks (Sept Chênes)
properties.description: Site of the 1816 battle known to the Métis as La battaille de la Grenouillère (Battle of Frog Plain). The conflict began when HBC Governor Semple's men opened fire on a Métis brigade led by Cuthbert Grant. The Métis victory is commemorated as a defining moment of national identity.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Shoal River (Shoal River House)
properties.description: An HBC post located on Pelican Lake, which was part of the Shoal River system. It served as a trade hub for the local Cree-Saulteaux Band and was connected to the settlement at Pelican Rapids.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Skownan, Rock Ridge and Mallard Métis Settlements
properties.description:
A cluster of settlements on the south shore of Waterhen Lake. Skownan is a Cree name meaning "to turn around the point." These communities were originally mixed Saulteaux-Métis bands that utilized the rich resources of the lake for hunting and fishing.

---

Associated with Skownan Band; named for a nearby lake. Settlers moved here in 1895 for wild game and Seneca root.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Smokey River
properties.description: The Smoky River is a major tributary of the Peace River in western Alberta, named for 'smouldering beds of coal in the riverbank' noted by the Cree. The river valley was home to many Métis families (Gladu, Joachim, Cardinal, Le Prêtre) documented in scrip applications from the 1870s–1890s, with settlements at Jasper House, Dunvegan, Peace River Landing, and Lesser Slave Lake.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Smokey River, Alberta Scrip Applications. Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: South Indian Lake Métis Settlement
properties.description: A community located 130 km north of Thompson. Originally home to a 1930s HBC post, the area was devastated by the Churchill River Diversion Project, which flooded the lake and ruined the traditional fishing and trapping economy.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Spring Creek (Lewistown)
properties.description: Located in Montana, this site is recognized for its first Catholic pioneers from the Métis community. It was a significant destination for Métis families migrating south from the Red River Settlement during the late 1800s.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.
properties.review_note: No supporting source found in local archive — Virtual Museum only

properties.name: St. Ambroise
properties.description: A small Métis community on the southeast tip of Lake Manitoba. After 1870, many Métis relocated here to "scrub land" that was independent and self-sufficient. The community relied heavily on the October whitefish run and harvesting saskatoon berries.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: St. Ambroise Dakota Entrenchment (Historic Site)
properties.description: A continuous circular earthen embankment 114 metres in diameter with interior rifle pits. It was built by a group of Dakota led by "The Leaf" who were fleeing bounty hunters in 1864. The site is a rare example of a ćunkaśke (Nation of the Forts) defensive structure.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: St. Andrews
properties.description: Located at the limestone rapids of the Red River south of Selkirk. It was chosen for early Indian and Métis settlements because the high ground offered protection from the massive floods that frequently devastated the Forks. The Selkirk Settlers later renamed the area St. Andrews.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: St. Boniface
properties.description:
Named by Bishop Provencher in honor of an English missionary. It was the historic center of the Red River Settlement and the administrative heart of the Métis Nation. Provencher famously said it would take "the faith of a Boniface" to work with the Métis.

---

The historic heart of the Red River Métis Homeland, this area served as the administrative, religious, and political center. St. Boniface was named by Bishop Provencher, who said it would take "the faith of a Boniface" to work with the Métis. The region is covered in multiple census databases (1901, 1911, 1916) and was the site of the Legislative Assembly of Assiniboia.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: St. Eustache (formerly Baie St. Paul)
properties.description: Originally part of the Baie St. Paul parish, the mission was destroyed by extensive flooding in 1882. Many residents relocated to North Dakota, but Bishop Tache sent missionary Thomas Quevillion to find a new, higher site. St. Eustache was established in January 1898 and named after the patron saint of hunters. In 1958, it was recorded as a large road allowance community with 434 Métis residents.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: St. François Xavier
properties.description: Established by Cuthbert Grant in 1823 as a buffer for the Red River Settlement. First industrial site in Manitoba for cart construction.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: St. Joseph (Walhalla)
properties.description: This location in North Dakota was where Father Belcourt moved his mission and school in 1853, thirty miles west of Pembina. It became a significant hub for Métis families who were forced to leave the Red River Settlement or Pembina due to international boundary shifts. It was also the home of John Tanner before he enlisted in the Union Army during the American Civil War.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: St. Laurent
properties.description: Established by Métis forced to leave Pembina when it became American territory. Residents maintained a strong Michif identity despite church and school pressure to adopt standard French.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: St. Mary’s (Portage la Prairie)
properties.description: Founded in 1853 by Reverend William Cockran and families from Middlechurch and St. Andrews. The settlers moved westward as the good river lots near the Forks were exhausted, seeking new farmland along the Assiniboine.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: St. Peter's Mission
properties.description: St. Peter's Mission (1859) was the third Jesuit mission in Montana, first located at Priest's Butte near Choteau, later moved to Sun River and then its present location west of Cascade. In 1883 Louis Riel taught a parochial school there, instructing Métis children and attending political meetings where Gabriel Azure was elected chairman. Many Métis at St. Peter's gave Riel power of attorney to sell their Canadian scrip rights. At its peak 500 children were taught at the mission.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. St. Peter's Mission. Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: St. Pierre
properties.description: St. Pierre (originally St. Pierre-Jolys) is a Métis wintering settlement on the Rat River in Manitoba, near the present-day community of St. Pierre-Jolys. The region was home to Métis families through the 19th century. The name 'Rivière St. Pierre' was also an early French explorer name applied to several rivers, including the Souris River, so named because they were discovered on the feast day of St. Peter.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: St. Vital
properties.description: Site of the early Métis river lots for families like the Riels, Naults, and Bruces. It developed along a broad elbow of the Red River south of St. Boniface. The Grey Nuns opened a school here in 1860.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Ste. Madeleine
properties.description: A traditional community of over twenty families homesteaded since the 1870s. In 1938, the town was destroyed to create a community pasture. Homes were burned, dogs were shot, and the church was dismantled.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Ste. Rose du Lac
properties.description: Established in 1889 by families from St. Vital looking for haylands to overwinter their animals. Led by the sons of Jean Baptiste Spence and guided by John Desmarais, they settled on the Turtle River.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Stony Mountain
properties.description: Known as "Snake Indian Hills," it was a high-ground sanctuary used for centuries to escape Red River floods. The "Faith Trail" brought settlers in ox-drawn carts to the region.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Stovepipe (Tuyau)
properties.description: A road allowance tent community in the Ste. Anne area inhabited by the families of woodcutters working in the forests to the east. The name "Stovepipe" referred to the makeshift stovepipes poking through the tent roofs.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Sugar Island (Lake Manitoba)
properties.description: A historic maple sugaring site for the Métis of Ebb and Flow and Bacon Ridge. Families like that of "Nonoonse" returned every spring for over forty years to continue the traditions of their grandmothers.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Summerberry Marsh
properties.description: A massive drainage basin east of The Pas used for muskrat trapping. The traditional livelihood was destroyed by the Grand Rapids Dam, leading to a long struggle for compensation by the Métis Federation.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Swan Creek
properties.description: Located north of St. Laurent near Monkman’s Point. It was inhabited primarily by the Monkman family and their relations, who eventually moved to St. Laurent to form a larger Métis hub.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Swan River (and Swan River House)
properties.description: Established in 1790 by the HBC and Cuthbert Grant. The post’s primary purpose was to trade for birch bark for canoe-making. Many modern Métis families trace their roots to the men who operated this post.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Tanner’s Crossing (Minnedosa)
properties.description: Founded by John Tanner, a veteran of the American Civil War, who built a ferry and store here on the Carlton Trail. The name was changed to Minnedosa in 1883 by land speculators.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Teton River
properties.description: The South Fork of the Teton River west of Choteau, Montana, flows through a canyon that was home to over 100 Métis, many of whom are buried in the old community cemetery maintained by the Wiseman family. Settlers like Jean-Baptiste Gariepy arrived in the canyon after 1885 to avoid U.S. forced removal to Canada, and were later joined by Métis refugees from the Riel Resistance. The Métis lived off the land, hunting, woodhawking, and collecting buffalo bones. Joseph Kinsey Howard conducted his research for Strange Empire while living among these Métis.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Teton River, South Fork. Louis Riel Institute.

properties.name: The Pas
properties.description: Rendezvous point for Cree and Métis hunters. Site of Devon Mission, established by Rev. Henry Budd in 1840.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: The Passage (Charleswood)
properties.description: A natural ford for buffalo and Red River carts where the Assiniboine River was shallow. It was used by independent traders to bypass the HBC monopoly at the Forks.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Thicket Portage
properties.description: Located on the Hudson Bay Railway line. Before the rail, the Métis here operated York boats and dog teams. The community was a key sturgeon fishing camp known as "The Fiddle."
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Thomas Métis Settlement
properties.description:
A road allowance community outside of The Pas named for the founding family. It was represented by the Northern Halfbreed Association starting in the 1930s.

---

A Métis road allowance community south of Binscarth. It was settled in 1939 by refugees from Ste. Madeleine after their original homes were burned. The community was sarcastically named "Selby Town" after one of the municipal officials the Métis blamed for their displacement.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Tin Town
properties.description: A road allowance community south of Rooster Town in Winnipeg. It was built from scrap metal and tin by destitute families during the Depression.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Tokyo at Crescent Lake
properties.description: A Métis community located near Crescent Lake in Saskatchewan. It is listed as one of the many dispersed settlements that formed after the migrations of the late 19th century.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Turtle Mountain (and Turtle Mountain House)
properties.description: A favorite wintering spot with abundant game. Turtle Mountain House was established in 1846 and operated by Antoine Desjarlais until 1855. It was a site of Métis resistance and starvation during the "winter of 1894."
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Umphreville or Humphreville (Settlement)
properties.description: A road allowance community northeast of The Pas named for its patriarch, John Umphreville. The family was connected to a vast kinship network extending from Moose Lake to Prince Albert.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Upper Athabasca
properties.description: This region's history is characterized as an Upper Athabasca Ethnohistory, involving the Snare, Snake, and Iroquois-Métis people. It was a key area for the northwestern fur trade and the Iroquois voyageur families who settled there.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.
properties.review_note: No supporting source found in local archive — Virtual Museum only

properties.name: Upper Fort Garry
properties.description: Network of narrow river lots extending north, home to hunters and farmers. Lots extended two miles east and west of river.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Wabowden
properties.description: Originally located at Setting Lake on the Grass River route, it relocated to Bowden Lake with the railway in the 1920s. It remains a major center for commercial fishing in the north.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Waterhen River (Rivière de la Poule d'Eau)
properties.description: The primary outflow for Lake Winnipegosis into Lake Manitoba. It was a vital transportation route used by Métis and Saulteaux hunters to move between St. Laurent and Duck Bay.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.

properties.name: Wekusko (Herb) Lake
properties.description: A mining supply center developed after gold was discovered in gold-bearing quartz in 1914. Former MMF President Ed Head worked here as a diamond-driller.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Wheat City
properties.description: A Métis community in Charleswood dating to the 1820s, located near a buffalo crossing. Families like the Branconniers and Morrissettes worked as harvest laborers and ferrymen.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: White Mud River Settlement (Rat Creek and Totogan)
properties.description: First named "White Mud River Settlement" after the color of the clay on its banks. It was a hunter's village with a small church and burial ground.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Whiteway Point
properties.description: A point on the narrows of Lake Winnipeg named after the HBC official in charge of the post. It is now called West Doghead Point.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Willow Bunch
properties.description: Historically known as Talle de Saules, this was a vital hivernant (wintering) community in Saskatchewan. It was a major destination for Métis families after the 1870 and 1885 Resistance movements.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.
properties.review_note: No supporting source found in local archive — Virtual Museum only

properties.name: Willow Flats
properties.description: A Métis road allowance community located in the Northwest Territories. It is part of the extensive northern network of Métis settlements characterized by displacement and landlessness.
properties.source: Virtual Museum of Métis History and Culture, Gabriel Dumont Institute of Native Studies and Applied Research.
properties.review_note: No supporting source found in local archive — Virtual Museum only

properties.name: Winnipegosis
properties.description: Named for the Cree word meaning "Little Muddy Water." It was the site of the 1741 Fort Dauphin and later a major center for the 20th-century timber and fishing booms.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: York Factory
properties.description: The central base of operations for the HBC fur trade for centuries. It featured a massive octagonal star fort designed to withstand naval attacks.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.

properties.name: Young Point Settlement
properties.description: A road allowance community south of The Pas in the Pasquia Settlement named for Josiah Young. It was excluded from town boundaries, denying residents basic services.
properties.source: Barkwell, Lawrence J. Historic Métis Settlements in Manitoba. Louis Riel Institute, July 2018.
